finding.vuln_claim: `direction` is interpolated into SQL without validation, enabling ORDER BY injection.
This endpoint performs a server-side fetch of attacker-controlled URLs, enabling SSRF.
Single replace('../','') does not stop path traversal, so crafted file values can escape /srv/data.
Untrusted cookie data is passed to `pickle.loads`, enabling arbitrary code execution.
Merging `req.body` into `user` lets attackers overwrite any writable field, including privileged flags.
Untrusted `name` is interpolated into a shell command and executed via `os.system`, enabling command injection.
The prefix check allows path traversal like `/var/data/../etc`, so attackers can back up files outside the intended directory.
Untrusted markdown is rendered to HTML without sanitization, enabling stored XSS in comments.
Negative amounts are accepted and turn a transfer into a self-credit plus victim debit.
The balance check and updates are not atomic, enabling concurrent overspend.
Production falls back to `jwt.decode`, which accepts any forged token without verifying the signature.
Any logged-in user can fetch another user's record by supplying a different `id`, with no ownership check.
The HMAC is compared with `==`, which leaks timing differences and can enable signature guessing.
The handler accepts any valid signed body without freshness or replay checks, so captured requests can be replayed.
The allowlist accepts `//evil.com`, so `redirect(nxt)` can send users to an external site.
XML parsing enables external entity resolution on untrusted request data, allowing XXE.
Unvalidated request fields are used directly in a database query, enabling NoSQL injection and auth bypass.
The URL allowlist is only a prefix check, so attackers can make the server fetch arbitrary HTTP(S) targets.
`gets(buf)` reads unlimited input into a 64-byte stack buffer.
The access token is stored in plaintext SharedPreferences.
The AES key is hardcoded, so anyone with source access can decrypt or forge protected data.
The pointer check is inverted, so a non-NULL out buffer is rejected and a NULL out buffer reaches memcpy/out[n] writes.
`direction` is interpolated into SQL without validation, enabling SQL injection in the `ORDER BY` clause.
The endpoint fetches an attacker-controlled URL with no allowlist, enabling SSRF.
The download endpoint is vulnerable to path traversal because a single `../` substring removal does not stop escaping `/srv/data`.
Untrusted cookie data is base64-decoded and passed to `pickle.loads`, enabling arbitrary code execution during deserialization.
`Object.assign(user, req.body)` lets callers overwrite arbitrary user fields.
Untrusted name is interpolated into a shell command and executed with os.system.
Untrusted comment body is rendered to HTML and stored without sanitization, enabling stored XSS.
Negative `amount` values are accepted, letting an attacker increase their own balance instead of transferring funds.
The balance check and updates are not atomic, so concurrent transfers can double-spend the same funds.
In production, invalid JWTs fall back to `jwt.decode`, which accepts attacker-controlled claims without verifying the signature.
Any authenticated user can query any `id` and receive that user's email, so this is an IDOR/broken authorization flaw.
Using `==` to compare HMACs leaks timing differences and can enable signature forgery.
The webhook accepts any valid body-signature pair without freshness checks, so captured requests can be replayed.
The `startswith('/')` check allows scheme-relative URLs like `//evil.com`, causing an open redirect.
Unsafe XML parsing allows XXE because external entities and network access are enabled on untrusted input.
Login input is copied into a database query, enabling NoSQL operator injection and auth bypass.
This endpoint performs a server-side fetch of attacker-controlled URLs, enabling SSRF to internal or cloud metadata hosts.
`gets(buf)` can overflow the 64-byte stack buffer because it has no bounds check.
The access token is stored in plaintext SharedPreferences.
The AES key is hardcoded, so anyone with source access can decrypt every ciphertext.
The guard is inverted, so valid outputs are rejected and a NULL `out` can reach `memcpy`, causing a crash.
`direction` is concatenated into SQL without validation, enabling SQL injection in the ORDER BY clause.
User-controlled `url` is fetched directly, enabling SSRF to arbitrary internal or external targets.
Single '../' stripping lets attackers traverse out of /srv/data and read arbitrary files.
User-controlled cookie data is deserialized with `pickle.loads`, enabling code execution if an attacker crafts a malicious `state` value.
The authorization gate does not bind the target `:id` to the caller, so a non-support user can patch any user record.
`Object.assign(user, req.body)` mass-assigns every client-supplied field onto the user object.
Untrusted `name` and `src` are interpolated into a shell command and executed with `os.system`.
`name` also controls the archive path, enabling path traversal outside `/tmp`.
Untrusted Markdown is rendered to HTML and stored without sanitization, enabling stored XSS.
Negative amounts are accepted, letting an attacker increase their own balance and debit the recipient.
The balance check and updates are non-atomic, so concurrent transfers can overspend the same funds.
Production falls back to `jwt.decode()` after signature verification fails, accepting unverified claims.
Any authenticated user can fetch another user's email by supplying an arbitrary id.
The HMAC check uses `==`, which can leak MAC prefix timing and allow signature forgery.
The prefix check allows scheme-relative URLs like `//evil.com`, creating an open redirect.
The parser resolves external entities, enabling XXE.
The endpoint performs an attacker-controlled server-side fetch, allowing SSRF to internal hosts and metadata services.
`gets(buf)` reads unbounded input into a 64-byte stack buffer.
The access token is stored in SharedPreferences without encryption, so it can be recovered from app storage.
The AES key is hardcoded in source, so anyone who reads the code can decrypt or forge all data protected by it.
The guard is inverted, so `out` can be NULL and then `memcpy` writes through it.
Untrusted `dir` is concatenated into SQL, enabling SQL injection in the ORDER BY clause.
Attacker-controlled `url` is fetched directly, making this an open SSRF proxy.
The filter only strips one "../" token, so crafted paths still traverse out of /srv/data.
Untrusted cookie data is deserialized with `pickle.loads`, enabling server-side code execution.
Untrusted req.body is merged into a persisted user, enabling attacker-controlled field overwrite.
Unquoted `name` and `src` are passed into `os.system`, enabling shell command injection.
Untrusted Markdown is rendered to HTML and stored without sanitization, enabling stored XSS.
Negative `amount` is accepted and flips the transfer into a balance-increasing mint/drain primitive.
The balance check and updates are non-atomic, so concurrent transfers can bypass the insufficient-funds check.
Failed JWT verification falls back to `jwt.decode`, so an attacker can send any forged token and still get claims accepted in production.
`//evil.com` passes the slash check and becomes an open redirect.
XML external entities are resolved on attacker-controlled input, enabling XXE.
User input is passed straight into a database query, enabling NoSQL injection and login bypass.
The endpoint fetches attacker-controlled http URLs, enabling SSRF to internal or metadata services.
gets(buf) can overflow the 64-byte stack buffer with attacker-controlled input.
The access token is written to SharedPreferences in plaintext.
The AES key is hardcoded, so anyone with the source can decrypt or forge ciphertext.
CBC mode is used without any integrity check, so ciphertexts are malleable.
The guard is inverted, so `out == NULL` can reach `memcpy` and crash the process.
Untrusted `dir` is concatenated into the SQL `ORDER BY`, enabling SQL injection.
`req.query.url` is fetched directly, enabling SSRF/open proxy to arbitrary hosts.
A crafted `file` like `/etc/passwd` or `../../x` can escape `/srv/data` because the filter only strips one literal `../` and `path.join` accepts absolute paths.
User-controlled `state` is passed to `pickle.loads`, enabling unsafe deserialization.
`Object.assign(user, req.body)` mass-assigns trusted fields, so attackers can set `role`, `isSupport`, or other protected attributes.
The endpoint updates any `req.params.id` without a verified ownership or admin check, enabling unauthorized profile tampering.
Unquoted `src` is passed to `os.system`, enabling shell injection and RCE.
Unquoted `name` is also interpolated into the shell command, enabling RCE.
Unsanitized Markdown is stored and later served as HTML, enabling stored XSS.
Negative amounts are accepted, letting an attacker credit their own balance and debit the recipient.
The balance update is a non-atomic read-modify-write race that can double-spend under concurrency.
Production falls back to jwt.decode() after verify() fails, so attacker-controlled tokens are trusted without a signature.
Any authenticated user can request any user ID and receive that account's email, which is an IDOR.
`//evil.com` bypasses the path check and becomes an external redirect.
The XML parser enables entity resolution with network access, allowing XXE and SSRF from attacker-supplied XML.
The login check is hardcoded true, so any request can pass authentication.
The prefix check still permits attacker-controlled HTTP(S) URLs, so this endpoint can SSRF internal services.
`gets(buf)` reads unbounded input into a 64-byte stack buffer.
The access token is stored in SharedPreferences as plain text.
The AES key is hardcoded in source, so anyone who reads the code can decrypt all past and future data.
CBC encryption is unauthenticated, so ciphertext can be modified without detection.
The NULL check is inverted, so a NULL `out` reaches `memcpy` and crashes.
Unvalidated `dir` is concatenated into the SQL `ORDER BY`, enabling SQL injection.
User-controlled `url` is fetched server-side without a positive allowlist, enabling SSRF.
Single replace only strips one ../, so crafted paths can still escape /srv/data.
Attacker-controlled cookie data is deserialized with pickle.loads, enabling code execution.
Any caller can patch any user by ID because the handler never verifies ownership before loading and saving the target record.
`name` is inserted into a shell command without quoting, enabling command injection.
`src` is also passed unquoted to the shell, and the prefix check does not prevent shell metacharacters.
Stored XSS via unsanitized Markdown HTML rendering.
Negative `amount` is accepted and turns a transfer into balance minting for the sender.
The balance check and updates are not atomic, enabling double-spend races.
Production falls back to unverified jwt.decode(), letting attackers supply trusted claims.
Using `==` to compare HMACs leaks timing differences and can let an attacker recover a valid signature.
`next` allows protocol-relative URLs like `//evil.com`, creating an open redirect.
XML is parsed with entity resolution and network access enabled, allowing XXE.
User-controlled `req.body` is used directly in a database filter, enabling NoSQL operator injection against login.
The endpoint is an SSRF sink because it fetches any `http` URL without an allowlist.
Unbounded gets(buf) can overflow the 64-byte stack buffer.
Access token is stored in plaintext SharedPreferences.
Hard-coded AES key lets anyone with source access decrypt or forge all ciphertexts.
The guard is inverted, so a NULL `out` pointer passes and gets dereferenced in `memcpy`.
